title: Developer Application and Drying
narrationScript: By the time you reach the developer station, all the evidence you need is already inside the part. The penetrant soaked into every flaw that is open to the surface, and the removal step stripped the excess off the top while leaving the flaws loaded. The trouble is that a loaded crack a fraction of a thousandth of an inch wide is still invisible. Developer is the step that turns trapped penetrant into an indication you can actually see. Think of a blotter. Press blotting paper against wet ink and the ink wicks up into the paper and spreads. Developer is a layer of fine white particles that does exactly that on the part surface. It contacts the penetrant sitting in the flaw, capillary action reverses direction, and the penetrant bleeds up and out into the developer layer. As it spreads, the indication grows far wider than the flaw itself. That magnification is the whole point. A crack you could never see becomes a line you cannot miss. The developer also does a second job. For visible red dye, it lays down a clean white background so the red stands out. For fluorescent penetrant, the particle layer scatters ultraviolet light and helps the dye glow brighter against a dark booth. There are several forms of developer, and your procedure tells you which one to use. Form a is dry powder, a light fluffy material applied in a dust chamber, with a gentle spray bulb, or by dipping. It is used with fluorescent penetrants only, because a thin dusting of powder gives no white background for a red dye to contrast against. Applied right it looks like almost nothing, a faint film clinging to a dry surface. If it cakes or piles up, the part was wet or you applied too much. Forms b and c are aqueous developers, carried in water. Form b dissolves in the water like sugar. Form c is a suspension of particles that must be kept agitated or it settles out. Both are applied to the part while it is still wet, right after the rinse, by dipping, flowing, or spraying, and then the whole part goes through the dryer where the coating dries to a uniform film. One caution worth remembering. Common practice restricts water soluble developer from use with water washable penetrants, because a water based coating can keep washing penetrant out of the very flaws you are trying to find. Forms d and e are nonaqueous wet developers, particles suspended in a fast drying solvent, usually in an aerosol can. They are the most sensitive form in common spot use. The solvent helps draw penetrant out, and the spray lays down a thin, even, fast drying film. They go on only after the part is completely dry. Shake the can until the mixing ball rattles freely, hold it eight to twelve inches away, and put down several light passes instead of one heavy coat. Form f covers special applications such as peelable film developers that lift a record of the indication. Drying has a place in the sequence, and the place depends on the form. With aqueous developers, you apply the developer first and dry after. With dry powder and nonaqueous developers, you dry the part first and apply developer to a dry surface. The dryer itself is usually a recirculating hot air oven, and a typical procedure limit keeps the part temperature at or below one hundred sixty degrees Fahrenheit. Use the minimum time that actually dries the part. Too hot or too long bakes the penetrant inside the flaws, drives off the dye, and your indications fade or vanish. Then comes the wait. Development time commonly runs at least ten minutes, and the clock starts when the developer is in place, meaning at application for dry powder and when the coating dries for the wet forms. Give the blotter time to work. But do not walk away forever. Indications keep spreading the longer they sit, and a crack read hours late turns into a wide fuzzy blob that hides its own shape. Apply the right form, in the right order, in a thin even film, dry it gently, give it its dwell, and read it inside the window. That is developer work done right, and the numbers in your written procedure always govern.
imagePrompt: Gloved technician shakes an aerosol can and sprays a thin even coat of white nonaqueous developer onto a dried machined steel valve body on an inspection bench, dwell timer and a rack of processed parts nearby.

sections.heading: What Developer Actually Does
sections.body:
By the time a part reaches the developer station, the test is already loaded. Penetrant has wicked into every surface-breaking flaw, and the removal step has stripped the excess without flushing out what is trapped inside. The problem is scale: a tight crack holding a microscopic volume of penetrant is still invisible. Developer makes it visible, three ways at once.

- **Blotting.** The developer film acts like blotting paper pressed against ink. It touches the penetrant at the flaw mouth and capillary action reverses — penetrant bleeds out of the flaw and spreads into the developer layer.
- **Magnification.** As the bleed-out spreads, the indication grows many times wider than the flaw that fed it. A crack a fraction of a thousandth of an inch wide becomes a line you can see across the bench.
- **Contrast.** For Type II visible penetrant, the developer lays down a white background that makes red dye jump out. For Type I fluorescent penetrant, the particle film scatters UV-A and helps the dye glow brighter against a dark booth.

No developer, weak developer, or a botched developer step means real flaws stay quiet. This is not the step to rush.

sections.caption: Developer works like a blotter: capillary action reverses and the bleed-out grows far wider than the flaw that feeds it.

sections.heading: The Developer Forms
sections.body:
Classification systems used by practices such as ASTM E1417 group developers into six forms. Your written procedure picks the form; your job is to know how each behaves.

- **Form a — dry powder.** A light, fluffy powder applied to a **dry** part in a dust chamber, fluidized bed, or with a gentle spray bulb. Used with **fluorescent penetrants only** — a thin dusting gives no white background for red dye to contrast against. Done right it is barely visible, a faint film that clings evenly.
- **Form b — water-soluble.** Particles dissolved in water like sugar. Applied to the still-wet part after rinsing, then dried to a film. Common practice restricts it from use with **water-washable penetrants** — a water-based coating can keep washing penetrant out of the flaws — and from Type II visible systems.
- **Form c — water-suspendible.** Insoluble particles suspended in water; the tank must be **agitated** or it settles. Applied wet, then dried. Usable with visible and fluorescent systems.
- **Forms d and e — nonaqueous wet.** Particles suspended in a fast-flashing solvent, usually in aerosol cans: form d for Type I, form e for Type II. Applied only to a **completely dry** surface. The most sensitive form in common spot use.
- **Form f — special applications**, such as peelable film developers that lift a permanent record of an indication.

sections.caption: Developer forms at a glance — typical practice; your written procedure governs.
rows: Dust chamber, bulb, or dip; lightest visible film; good on rough surfaces
Generally not with water-washable or Type II
d with Type I, e with Type II
Aerosol; most sensitive common form; thin passes; flammable
Peelable film and other engineered uses

sections.heading: Applying It Right
sections.body:
**Dry powder** wants a dry part and a light touch. Create a cloud and let it settle, or puff it from a bulb, then gently shake or blow off the excess with low-pressure air. If powder cakes or piles, the surface was wet or you applied far too much — caked powder hides indications instead of developing them.

**Aqueous developers** go on by dip, flow, or spray straight after the final rinse, while the part is wet. Drain the part so the coating dries thin and uniform — pooled developer in pockets and recesses dries thick and masks whatever is underneath. Check bath concentration per the procedure, and keep suspendible baths agitated.

**Nonaqueous developer** is aerosol work. Shake the can until the mixing ball rattles freely. Hold it roughly 8 to 12 inches from the surface and lay down several light passes, letting each flash off, rather than one heavy wet coat. The target on a fluorescent job is a thin, almost translucent film; on a visible job, an even white layer just opaque enough to give contrast. In both cases the coat should look almost too thin — that is correct. Aerosol solvents are flammable: ventilation and ignition control per the safety data sheet and shop rules.

sections.title: The thin-film rule
sections.body: Developer fails in both directions. **Too little** and there is nothing to blot penetrant out of the flaw and nothing to build contrast. **Too much** and the indication has to fight up through the coating — fine indications never make it. Aim for the thinnest film that does the job, and let the bleed-out, not the developer, be what you notice.

sections.heading: Drying: Order and Limits
sections.body:
Where the dryer falls in the sequence depends entirely on the developer form:

- **Aqueous (b, c):** rinse → apply developer wet → **then dry**. The oven dries part and coating together.
- **Dry powder (a) and nonaqueous (d, e):** rinse → **dry first** → apply developer to the dry surface. Powder will not cling properly to a wet part, and solvent developer over a wet surface traps water under the film.

The dryer is typically a recirculating hot-air oven. Typical procedure limits hold **part temperature at or below about 160 °F** and keep time to the minimum that actually dries the part. Heat is not your friend here: an oven set too hot, or a part left in too long, evaporates the penetrant sitting in the flaws — the bake-out that turns a cracked part into a clean-looking one. Drain or blow off standing water before the oven to shorten dryer time, and let hot parts settle back toward the working temperature band before solvent-based developer goes on.

sections.caption: The dryer's position in the sequence depends on the developer form: aqueous developers are applied wet then dried; dry powder and nonaqueous forms require a dry part first.

sections.heading: Development Time and the Reading Window
sections.body:
Development time is a real dwell, not a formality — bleed-out takes time. Typical procedure minimums run **at least 10 minutes**. When the clock starts depends on the form:

- **Dry powder:** at application.
- **Aqueous and nonaqueous:** when the coating is **dry**, because blotting only works once the film has formed.

There is also a maximum, set by your procedure, because indications never stop growing. Left too long, bleed-out spreads wide and fuzzy: two close indications merge into one blob, a sharp linear loses the shape that identifies it, and sizing becomes guesswork. Plan the batch so every part is read inside its window — and if a window is missed, the fix is not a quick re-spray. The part is cleaned and fully reprocessed. These are typical values throughout; the written procedure governs.

figures.title: How developer draws penetrant out of a crack
Where the dryer fits: two paths

checkQuestions.question: A procedure calls for Type II visible penetrant on structural weldments. Why is dry powder developer (form a) the wrong choice?
options: Dry powder is too sensitive for welds and produces excessive background
Dry powder reacts chemically with red dye and bleaches it
A thin dusting of powder provides no white contrast background, which visible red dye depends on
Dry powder can only be applied by dipping, which weldments do not allow
checkQuestions.explanation: Dry powder is used with fluorescent systems only. Visible red dye needs an opaque white background for contrast, and a thin powder film cannot provide one.

checkQuestions.question: Using a water-suspendible developer (form c) on a water-washable fluorescent line, where does the dryer fall in the sequence?
options: Immediately after the rinse, before the developer goes on
After the developer is applied — the part and the coating dry together
Before the penetrant dwell, then again before inspection
Drying is unnecessary because the developer is water-based
checkQuestions.explanation: Aqueous developers are applied to the still-wet part right after rinsing; the dryer then forms the uniform dry film. Drying first applies to dry powder and nonaqueous forms.

checkQuestions.question: An inspector lays a heavy, fully opaque coat of nonaqueous developer over a suspect area 'to be sure.' What is the most likely outcome?
options: Sensitivity improves because more solvent enters the flaw
Development time can safely be shortened
The white coat makes UV inspection unnecessary
Fine indications are buried — bleed-out cannot fight up through a thick coating
checkQuestions.explanation: Developer must be a thin, even film. A heavy coat masks fine indications because the small volume of penetrant in a tight flaw cannot migrate through the full coating thickness.

checkQuestions.question: Parts come out of a dryer that was running hot, well above the typical 160 °F part-temperature limit, after a long soak. What is the process risk?
options: Penetrant trapped in the flaws dries out and loses its dye — indications fade or never form
The developer becomes more adherent and over-develops
Water spots etch the surface and create false linear indications
There is no risk — heat affects only the developer, not the penetrant
checkQuestions.explanation: Excess heat evaporates the penetrant and degrades the dye sitting in the flaws — the bake-out failure. Dryers are kept at the lowest effective temperature and time.

checkQuestions.question: Using nonaqueous developer, when does development time officially begin?
options: When the aerosol can is first shaken
When the part exits the dryer
When the sprayed film has dried on the surface
Ten minutes after the penetrant dwell ends
checkQuestions.explanation: For wet developer forms the development clock starts once the coating is dry, because the blotting action needs a formed film. For dry powder it starts at application.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §2.5 (developer application and drying)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, liquid penetrant testing practice (developer forms and use, general reference)
ASTM E165/E165M, liquid penetrant examination practice (general reference)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6, liquid penetrant examination (general reference)
